figures.caption: Table 3: Cross-model validation at full-BEIR scale on five monolithic embedding-model families across a tenfold parameter range: e5-small-v2 at 33M, e5-base-v2 at 110M, e5-large-v2 at 335M, gte-base-en-v1.5 at 110M, and bge-large-en-v1.5 at 335M. The table reports ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) of SOFTCENTROID default K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 and two FiQA-tuned variants. Significance: ∗ p < 0.05, † p < 0.01, ‡ p < 0.001 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10, reported on the four cells with stat-sig analysis. NFCorpus and ArguAna lift positively and statistically significantly on the e5 and gte families with the default at ≥ 100M. The algebra also extends to e5-small-v2 at 33M with no model-quality penalty, with ArguAna at +8.13, around seven times larger than the corresponding bge-small-en-v1.5 gain at the same 33M. Combined with the j-v5 results in Table 1, default SOFTCENTROID produces positive ∆ on NFCorpus and ArguAna across all seven embedding-model families tested at full-BEIR scale, namely j-v5-small, j-v5-nano, e5-small-v2, e5-base-v2, e5-large-v2, gte-base-en-v1.5, and bge-large-en-v1.5. bge-base-en-v1.5 is the only≥ 100M case where the algebra is partial.

Figure 12: Program P19 (first-sentence anchor). Same skeleton as P1, but the top-1 document is truncated to its first sentence and re-encoded. The resulting first-sentence vector replaces the document anchor. The 2×-cost frontier holder on ArguAna among j-v5 programs.

Figure 11: Program P1 (top-1 self-amplification). Encodes query and document under the retrieval LoRA, finds the top-1 document by cosine similarity, then updates the query as q′ = L2(q+ w d(1)) with w = cos(q,d(1)), and reranks. The minimal embedding-space PRF and founder of the AMP family. Cost 1×.

Figure 13: Program P22 (four-LoRA majority gate). Re-encodes the query under all four task-specialized adapters for retrieval, classification, clustering, and text-matching and admits the amp only if at least three LoRAs agree on top-1. A representative LORAFUSION-substrate program. It does not enter the universal frontier because hard-vote over heterogeneous LoRAs averages away the signal that any single LoRA carries.

Table 7: Best per-(family, task) ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) on j-v5-small. Bold marks the all-family winner per column. § marks not applicable, since DOCCHUNK requires long structured documents and ArguAna and FiQA lack this.

Figure 6: Cost-accuracy Pareto frontier over all 259 registered programs. SOFTCENTROID holds the 1× corner and FIRSTSENT-anchor variants hold the ≤ 2× corner. Nothing above 2× moves the universal frontier.

Figure 7: Test-time scaling of dense retrieval. Each blue point is one of 240 programs with at least four cells evaluated, plotted at its cost factor c, expressed as encoder forward passes per query, against its mean ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) across the four BEIR tasks and both j-v5-small and j-v5-nano. The red step-curve is the Pareto upper envelope. The Pareto frontier rises sharply from +0.00 at 1× to +2.62 at 2× and saturates at +2.67 near 4×. Cost factors above 4× produce no further improvement.

Figure 22: Program P102 (gap-concentration amp). Replaces the gap-confidence gate with a top-K concentration measure. The amp fires when the top-K similarity mass is concentrated above a threshold. A robustness-witness variant that maintains the AMP body but tightens the admit set.

Figure 21: Program P97 (top-2 chunked anchor). Uses both the top-1 and top-2 documents’ first sentences as anchors, max-pooling their similarities to the query before reranking. Captures the highest nano-SciFact delta of +0.51 nDCG@10×100 among 2×-cost programs but breaks ArguAna because the top-2 is often a counter-argument with high cosine.

Figure 3: Cumulative best universal ∆nDCG@10 averaged over six cells, as a function of generation. The curve flattens after generation 30 and saturates after generation 78, suggesting the 2× universal frontier is exhausted under the current encoder API.

Figure 19: Program P47 (cross-LoRA top-3 overlap gate). Fires the amp only when the retrieval-LoRA and the text-matching-LoRA agree on the top-3 set. Established the cross-LoRA agreement pattern. Subsequent programs P49 and P110 extended this gate to triple-LoRA composition.

Figure 20: Program P76 (universal-min champion at 2×). Combines first-sentence anchor with a similarity-floor gate. The longest-defended 2×-cost universal-positive in the registry, with +0.29 nDCG@10 ×100 minimum across all six universal cells.

Figure 28: Program P77 (doc-chunk K=2 redundancy gate). Splits the top-1 document into four disjoint sentencewindow chunks and encodes each separately. The amp fires only if at least two chunks have cos(q, chunki) above the per-query median similarity over the top-100 pool. Representative of the DOCCHUNK substrate family. Cost ≥ 4×.

Table 2: Small-sample generalization probe with the default SOFTCENTROID at K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 frozen across all 13 nanoBEIR retrieval tasks on both models. NanoBEIR is a 50-query random sub-sample of each BEIR task. Paired-bootstrap p-values use 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. At this sample size, per-cell p-values are noisy. Deltas that conflict in sign with the full-corpus evaluation, for example ArguAna on j-v5-nano at −2.16 here against +3.19 on the full 1,406-query corpus in Table 1, are non-significant at p=.07. Bold marks the single cell significant at p < .05. HotpotQA on j-v5-small at−3.24, p=.014 is the only statistically significant regression across both models. On j-v5-nano no cell crosses the 95% bar.

Figure 27: Program P28 (norm-convergence amp). Iterates the top-1 amp and tracks the update norm ∥qi+1 − qi∥. Stops the moment the norm increases (convergence reversal), with a safety cap of six rounds. Oracle-best on j-v5-nano ArguAna at +7.18 nDCG@10 ×100. Cost ≤ 2×.

Figure 30: Program P203 (per-query lambda). Uses two LoRA adapters: the retrieval LoRA for the initial retrieval and centroid, and the text-matching LoRA for a per-query interaction signal. The sign of the Pearson correlation between the two LoRA similarity vectors determines whether the centroid update adds or subtracts. Oracle-best on j-v5-nano NFCorpus at +1.69 nDCG@10 ×100. Cost 2×.

Table 11: Held-out full-BEIR cross-model validation, reporting ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) versus the cosine baseline. The SOFTCENTROID default uses K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05. Fusion uses BM25 score-level mix at w=0.1, defined in Section 5.6. Significance: ‡ p < 10−3, † p < 10−2, ∗ p < 5 · 10−2 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. All four models on SCIDOCS show stat-sig SOFTCENTROID default lift, with p ≤ 0.001 on three of four. On Touche2020 the fusion lever provides p < 10−3 compensatory lift on all four models. SCIDOCS is multi-relevance scientific IR where the centroid update directly captures relevance. Touche2020 is argument retrieval where dense baselines trail BM25 and the fusion lever provides compensatory lexical signal.

Figure 18: Program P40 (multi-round Rocchio). Iterates the centroid update for T > 1 rounds, re-retrieving after each query update. Ruled out: rounds beyond T = 1 drift the query off the document manifold and lose ranking accuracy. The search moved beyond the multi-round-PRF substrate early.

Table 13: SOFTCENTROID component ablation, reporting ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) over the cosine baseline at each one-parameter sweep around the default K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05. Interpolation α and temperature τ carry most of the gain. Centroid size K is largely inert.

Table 4: SOFTCENTROID versus best classical Rocchio on full-BEIR ArguAna across all seven embeddingmodel families. Each value is ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) over the cosine baseline. The Rocchio number is the best of a sixteen-cell (K,β) grid. SC uses the universal default K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05. All 14 methodmodel cells are statistically significant at p < 10−4 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples. The softmaxweighted variant exceeds the uniform-mean baseline on every model by +2.31 to +6.00 nDCG@10.

Figure 15: Program P32 (tail-gap weighted amp). Replaces the cosine weight in P1 with a similarity-tail gap w = sim(1) − sim(K), so amplification scales with the confidence margin between top-1 and the rest of the top-K. Member of the centroid family.

Figure 16: Program P33 (similarity-floor first-sent amp). Adds a lower bound on cosine before the amp fires. Below the floor the program returns the cosine baseline. An early gating-axis exploration that established the gap-confidence pattern reused by later universal champions.

Table 1: Headline frontier results, reporting nDCG@10 ×100. Top block: oracle-best per-cell frontier program discovered by the meta-program from 10,535 evaluated configurations on the four-task nanoBEIR slices used to gate the loop’s frontier admission. Middle block: default SOFTCENTROID at K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 applied with no per-task tuning, on full BEIR for the j-v5 family. Bottom block: cross-model full-BEIR validation on e5-base-v2. Significance: ‡ p < 0.001, † p < 0.01 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. “Cost” counts encoder forward passes. § Nano-slice FiQA gains do not survive at full-BEIR scale on the j-v5 family. The full-corpus FiQA evidence is on e5-base-v2 in the bottom block at +0.47.

Table 16: Cross-model transfer on the 4-task nanoBEIR probe at the 50-query slice. The table reports ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) of SOFTCENTROID default and two FiQA-tuned variants on three monolithic embedding models. The full-BEIR validation in Table 3 uses e5-base-v2 as the strongest of these three. Bold marks positive cells.

Figure 24: Program P164 (query-projection rotation). Projects the query onto the top-K subspace via the percoefficient mean of ⟨q,di⟩ weights. A linear-algebraic alternative to the centroid update. Non-universal because over-rotation destabilizes SciFact ordering.

Figure 23: Program P103 (two-round first-sent amp). A multi-round member of the AMP family that re-retrieves with q′ before the second amp pass. Like P40, the second round drifts the query off-manifold. The search moved beyond this direction.

Figure 2: Four representative programs discovered by the agentic loop. (a) P1: top-1 self-amp, founder of the AMP family, cost 1×. (b) P32: tail-gap weighted amp, SOFTCENTROID family, cost 1×. (c) P19: first-sent anchor, 2×-cost frontier holder on ArguAna. (d) P35: dropout ensemble, stochastic sampling baseline, cost M×. Programs (a)–(c) belong to the centroid super-family; (d) mean-pools unit-norm embeddings under dropout.
